when_to_use: intimate to flowing bossa nova, nylon guitar batida, straight 16ths with a laid-back lead, saudade-leaning AABA arc, 80-160 BPM

defaults.feel: rhythm section dead straight 16ths (no swing), lead/vocal laid-back or rubato, bass anticipation into chord changes

signature_moves: Jobim chromatic descent: Cmaj7 -> C#dim7 -> Dm7 -> D#dim7 -> C/E -> A7(b9,b13)
major-dominant II (Ipanema formula): Fmaj7 -> G7(13) -> Gm9 -> C7(b9)
minor line cliche (saudade): Am9 -> Bm7b5 -> E7(b9,b13) -> Am(maj7) Am6
backdoor + tritone sub: Cmaj9 -> Fm9 Bb13 -> Cmaj7 -> Db13
cadence_options: I - VI - ii - V: Cmaj9 -> A7(b9) -> Dm9 -> G13 G7(b13)
tag ending: vamp the last 2 bars 3x, land on a strummed 6/9 left to ring
avoid: plain triads (C, Dm, G) - always use at minimum a 7th/9th/6-9 (see classic-jazz-genre.md vocabulary contrast in bossa-nova-genre.md SS4)
swinging the rhythm section - see bossa-nova-genre.md SS2/SS8, this is a straight-grid genre

hook_archetypes.name: one-note-samba
hook_archetypes.rhythm: a single held or lightly repeated melody note while the harmony underneath moves chromatically
hook_archetypes.contour: flat/minimal pitch motion on the lead, all the motion happens in the chord line
hook_archetypes.first_appears: Verse A1, over the Jobim chromatic descent

hook_archetypes.name: call-and-batida-response
hook_archetypes.rhythm: 2 bars of lead melody, then 2 bars of lead silence filled by a denser guitar batida
hook_archetypes.contour: the guitar's response quotes the melody's rhythm, not its pitch

intro.note_lengths: sparse or absent - the batida states the groove alone

verse.note_lengths: sotto voce, mid-length phrases, target extended tones (9th/11th/13th) on chord changes
verse.placement: laid-back, behind the straight rhythm-section grid

bridge.note_lengths: slightly longer, more harmonically active phrases
bridge.contour: follows the denser altered-dominant harmony, still restrained
bridge.placement: laid-back, dynamics lift slightly (pp to mp)

solo.note_lengths: freer, more chromatic passing tones over the verse changes
solo.contour: sub-tone tenor sax or flute style - breathy, understated virtuosity, not aggressive
solo.placement: rubato phrase edges over an unwaveringly straight rhythm section

drum_skeleton.low_energy: thin cross-stick echoing the batida, shaker constant 16ths, surdo muted on beat 1
drum_skeleton.mid_energy: cross-stick and shaker unchanged (bossa density is stable, not additive like funk/neo-soul), surdo adds a soft accent on beat 3
drum_skeleton.high_energy: optional pandeiro/agogo layered in for the up-tempo samba-bossa zone (135-160 BPM), still straight 16ths, never swung

arrangement_defaults.layout_rules: guitar batida opens solo; shaker enters bar 3, bass enters bar 5 on root-5th; lead enters at Verse A1 sotto voce; counter-melody (flute/Rhodes) and string pad enter at Verse A2, very soft; bridge modulates and thickens harmony; re-head (A3) is the fullest texture; solo interlude sits over the verse changes; outro strips to a vamped tag ending on a 6/9 chord left to ring

anti_boredom_rules: the rhythm section must never swing, at any energy level - straight 16ths throughout (see groove_profile)
only the lead/vocal lays back or goes rubato - if the guitar/bass/percussion also drag, the arrangement has drifted into lofi/neo-soul territory
use at least one signature_move (chromatic descent, backdoor+tritone sub, or minor line cliche) - do not stay on the plain I-VI-ii-V the whole tune
the bridge must modulate or otherwise contrast harmonically - it cannot just repeat the verse progression
keep dynamics restrained (pp-mp for most of the form) - a bossa arrangement that gets loud/dense everywhere has lost the genre's restraint
